content:
Hi everyone I was redirected here by Trevor and had some questions about how you guys did things.

  1. is the Rust for Linux Github a mirror? Or is code only sent through mailing lists?
  2. What exactly is the mission? I assume something like Mircosoft's windows or windows-sys crates. Also safe vs raw bindings
  3. Where do I start, a lot of the "good first issues" on Github are already covered and have pr's ready for them.
Also I would also like to sharpen my c skills in other areas of the kernel but it's a little intimidating. Rust is something I've been doing for almost a year now.

Hey, thanks for visiting

Redhawk said:

Hi everyone I was redirected here by Trevor and had some questions about how you guys did things.

  1. is the Rust for Linux Github a mirror? Or is code only sent through mailing lists?

The RFL Github has the branch rust-next, which is staging for the next version of the kernel. It also has PRs which are more or less a tracker for what everyone is working on, and a place to collect feedback. Actual changes need to go through the mailing list (where, if accepted, they will be applied to rust-next or the relevant subsystem's branch). Some more info here: https://rust-for-linux.com/contributing

  1. What exactly is the mission? I assume something like Mircosoft's windows or windows-sys crates. Also safe vs raw bindings

The separation is a bit different on Windows. I think that windows-sys is mostly about writing userspace programs. RFL is about being able to write kernel things in Rust, like drivers, file systems, graphics, network traffic control, etc. There is no Rust API exposed for kernel syscalls, at least not at this time :)

Windows is starting to add rust to their kernel https://www.thurrott.com/windows/282471/microsoft-is-rewriting-parts-of-the-windows-kernel-in-rust but it's not public. The most fair public comparison would probably be what you can do with https://github.com/microsoft/windows-drivers-rs is roughly what you could do with a Linux out of tree module https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/rust-out-of-tree-module.

  1. Where do I start, a lot of the "good first issues" on Github are already covered and have pr's ready for them.

I wouldn't trust the good first issue label, I think it just hasn't been a lot of places. If you're scanning through the issues and see something of interest, just ask to get a better idea of how complex it is.

Another good way to jump in is to think of a project that interests you. The most supported thing in-tree at this point is phy drivers, and they are really pretty simple - maybe you would like to reimplement the one for your computer's phys as a getting started project. Or look through the different kernel subsystems and see if anything interests you.

If you are just interested in helping with RFL but not contributing to the kernel specifically, there are a lot of things across rustc and bindgen that are needed too and can be a bit easier to get started with: https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/issues/2

Trevor Gross said:

Hey, thanks for visiting

So where do actual code changes go? I've only used sites like Github or Gitlab where you just fork the project and you have your own remote point for git to push anything to, and then you create pull requests on the website ui. I have the rc tree cloned but I wasn't sure if you guys have your own remote I should use.

And how do I build/test this project? do I really just do cd rust && cargo build?

Also the scope of rfl is to write bindings for all public kernel api's?

Redhawk said:

Trevor Gross said:

Hey, thanks for visiting

So where do actual code changes go? I've only used sites like Github or Gitlab where you just fork the project and you have your own remote point for git to push anything to, and then you create pull requests on the website ui. I have the rc tree cloned but I wasn't sure if you guys have your own remote I should use.

Did you read https://rust-for-linux.com/contributing and follow the links to https://docs.kernel.org/process/index.html? If yes and you still have questions, I will gladly elaborate :)

See what Andreas linked about changes. They show up here, anything with [PATCH] https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/. You still fork the project for your own work

And how do I build/test this project? do I really just do cd rust && cargo build?

Not quite so simple. The entire kernel is built with make, as are the rust components https://www.kernel.org/doc/html/latest/rust/quick-start.html

Also the scope of rfl is to write bindings for all public kernel api's?

All is a big word. It is nice to grow the number of areas there are abstractions for, but it only makes sense to add them as they are needed to accomplish tasks (new drivers or functionality)

Trevor Gross said:

they are needed to accomplish tasks (new drivers or functionality)

So ie something like kernel modules could be wrote in rust, minus the headache rust has with abi's. Thanks for the tips about building, I haven't actually looked at the makefile for the kernel.

also thanks for the quickstart guide, I can find a lot of info about compiling the kernel, but not about the rust part lol. Just before I create a git migraine, fork the rust-for-linux github and submit features to rust-next. With bug fixes going to main? The contribution page doesn't have any of that info about the tree for this subgroup.

Redhawk said:

Trevor Gross said:

they are needed to accomplish tasks (new drivers or functionality)

So ie something like kernel modules could be wrote in rust, minus the headache rust has with abi's. Thanks for the tips about building, I haven't actually looked at the makefile for the kernel.

also thanks for the quickstart guide, I can find a lot of info about compiling the kernel, but not about the rust part lol. Just before I create a git migraine, fork the rust-for-linux github and submit features to rust-next. With bug fixes going to main? The contribution page doesn't have any of that info about the tree for this subgroup.

You don't need rust-for-linux github, unless you are working on something that requires the changes in rust-next. You can build your changes on top of latest Linux release.
For instance, the block device patches are on top of v6.7 from kernel.org
Figure out how to build and configure your kernel first (any branch), don't worry about Rust until you have that working :smile:
Fwiw, git clone ... --depth 1000 will save you some of that headache by only pulling the last 1000 commits (instead of all 1.2M)

Redhawk said:

Also I would also like to sharpen my c skills in other areas of the kernel but it's a little intimidating. Rust is something I've been doing for almost a year now.

If I were to tell you what helped me out getting familiarized with the kernel, it was studying and working with userspace components. You can learn a lot of things that will apply in kernelspace, for example if you want to use the kernel crypto API, getting to know cryptography in general and libraries like OpenSSL or RustTLS would be a prior requirement. Another example would be USB, you learn about some things of the USB spec plus the libusb API and you'll be ready for using it in the kernel.
Really, userspace maps very well with the kernel.

Trevor Gross said:

Fwiw, git clone ... --depth 1000 will save you some of that headache by only pulling the last 1000 commits (instead of all 1.2M)

Okay thank you so much, my last question is how you guys stage patchs, what I've read before is all new features go into the next rc ie 6.8, and all bug fixes go into 6.7 code. Is that how you guys do it? I know every subsystem does it differently, so I wanted to ask
Depends on the patch. Rust-only things to on top of rust-next (on the GH), but other patches go through the relevant subsystem
You are safe starting any work on top of the 6.7 tag, or the 6.8-rc tags once available
and when i want to make a pr to you guys, where would i post my code? like a normal github pr?
That is a good place for initial review

Trevor Gross said:

Fwiw, git clone ... --depth 1000 will save you some of that headache by only pulling the last 1000 commits (instead of all 1.2M)

Fwiw git clone --filter blob:none is useful too, but it comes with a price though

Trevor Gross said:

That is a good place for initial review

So I went through the first page of issues on Github and most seem to be finished or are in review by other groups. Aka basically closed, but they all seem to be open still. I'm having trouble finding a good issue to work on.
In case you are interested in some literature about the Linux kernel, this may be a good reading for starting : https://sysprog21.github.io/lkmpg/
